props.command: when should I NOT use you?
output: the first draft of anything you're learning to draft
facts you won't verify, decisions you'd hide

props.heading: Use it for leverage.
props.body: Quizzing yourself on your own material — that's retrieval practice, the most evidence-backed study technique there is. Getting feedback on a draft you wrote yourself. Asking for explanations of concepts you're stuck on. These uses amplify your own work without replacing it.

props.heading: Don't use it for: graded work without disclosure — that's the line the policy be
props.body: Don't use it to generate practice problems you won't actually work through. And don't use it to skip the struggle on something where the struggle is the learning: the first draft of an essay, the first pass at a problem, the initial attempt at a skill.

props.heading: The one question that collapses all the edge cases: would you hide this use from
props.body: If you'd be comfortable saying 'I used Claude to quiz myself on this material before the exam,' that's a legitimate use. The test isn't about what Claude can do — it's about what you'd disclose.

props.heading: Commit before the reveal: which of these four is a don't-use? A: ask Claude to q
props.body: B: ask Claude to write your essay. C: ask Claude to explain a concept you don't understand from lecture. D: ask Claude to suggest three examples you could use in your essay.

props.heading: B.
props.body: Writing your essay removes the drafting — which is the skill the assignment is developing. The other three are all legitimate uses: quizzing, explaining, and suggesting examples you then evaluate and use or reject are amplifiers of your work, not replacements for it.

artifactLines: USE for: quizzing yourself, feedback on YOUR draft, explanations of confusing concepts, practice generation, planning.
DON'T USE for: graded work without disclosure, first drafts you're supposed to learn by drafting, unverified facts, anything you'd hide.
The one question: would you hide this use from your teacher or boss? If yes — don't. Not fear of being caught. The hiding means the use is taking something that was supposed to be yours.
Knowing when not to use it IS the AI skill.

props.command: Give me a short wallet-card version of when I should and shouldn't use AI as a [student / mid-career professional]. Max 6 bullet points total. Practical and direct.
